title: The Hunchback of Notre-Dame by Victor Hugo

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Paris: The City of Notre-Dame
informationalTexts.text: In the Middle Ages, Paris was very different from the modern city we know today. The streets were narrow and dirty. People threw their garbage and waste right into the streets! There were no cars — instead, people walked or rode horses. Notre-Dame Cathedral stood at the center of the city on an island in the Seine River. The cathedral took nearly 200 years to build, from 1163 to 1345. It was the tallest building in Paris and could be seen from miles away. On Sunday mornings, the bells would ring so loudly that everyone in the city could hear them. The cathedral was not just a church — it was a gathering place, a shelter, and a symbol of the city itself.
questions: How long did it take to build Notre-Dame Cathedral?
What river is Notre-Dame built on?
Why was Notre-Dame important to medieval Paris beyond being a church?

informationalTexts.title: Victor Hugo: The Writer Who Saved Notre-Dame
informationalTexts.text: Victor Hugo was born in France in 1802. He grew up to become one of France's most famous writers. When Hugo was young, Notre-Dame Cathedral was falling apart. The French Revolution had damaged it, and people didn't care about fixing old buildings. Hugo loved Notre-Dame and was heartbroken to see it crumbling. So in 1831, he wrote The Hunchback of Notre-Dame to make people fall in love with the cathedral again. His plan worked! After the novel became popular, France spent millions of francs restoring Notre-Dame. Hugo's book literally saved the cathedral. He died in 1885, and over 2 million people attended his funeral — one of the largest funerals in history.
questions: What year did Victor Hugo publish The Hunchback of Notre-Dame?
Why did Hugo write this story?
How many people attended Hugo's funeral in 1885?

informationalTexts.title: Gothic Architecture: Building to the Sky
informationalTexts.text: Notre-Dame is built in a style called Gothic architecture. Gothic cathedrals have several special features: pointed arches, tall towers, huge stained-glass windows, and flying buttresses (stone supports on the outside that keep the walls from collapsing). Medieval builders wanted their churches to reach toward heaven, so they built them as tall as possible. The inside of a Gothic cathedral is designed to make you feel small and amazed. Light streams through colorful windows, creating patterns on the floor. The ceilings soar so high above you that your voice echoes. Building a Gothic cathedral required hundreds of workers and took decades — sometimes over a century — to complete.
questions: What are flying buttresses and what do they do?
Why did medieval builders make cathedrals so tall?
How long did Gothic cathedrals typically take to build?

informationalTexts.title: Bells and Bell Ringers
informationalTexts.text: Before clocks, radios, or phones, church bells told people the time and warned them of danger. Bell ringers had one of the most important jobs in the city. They rang the bells for morning prayers, noon, evening, and midnight. They also rang bells to warn of fires, floods, or attacks. The largest bell in Notre-Dame is called Emmanuel. It weighs over 13 tons — as much as nine cars! Ringing such heavy bells was exhausting work. Bell ringers pulled on thick ropes with all their strength. The vibrations were so loud that many bell ringers lost their hearing over time. In Hugo's story, Quasimodo loves the bells because they are the only 'voices' he can still hear.
questions: What did church bells tell people before modern technology?
How much does Notre-Dame's largest bell, Emmanuel, weigh?
Why did many bell ringers lose their hearing?

informationalTexts.title: The Festival of Fools
informationalTexts.text: The Festival of Fools was a real medieval tradition held every January. For one day, the rules of society were turned upside down. Poor people dressed as nobles. Servants gave orders to their masters. The festival often included electing a 'Pope of Fools' or 'King of Fools' — usually the ugliest or silliest person in the crowd. The festival allowed common people to make fun of the church and powerful leaders without getting in trouble. It was chaotic, loud, and wild. Many church officials hated the Festival of Fools and tried to ban it, but the tradition was so popular that it continued for centuries. Hugo uses this festival in his novel to show how quickly a crowd's love can turn to cruelty.
questions: When was the Festival of Fools traditionally held?
What happened to social rules during the festival?
Why did church officials dislike the Festival of Fools?

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Justice: Cruel and Public
informationalTexts.text: In medieval times, punishments were harsh and public. If you stole bread, you might lose a hand. If you lied in court, your tongue could be cut out. The pillory was a common punishment: a wooden frame that locked a person's head and hands in place while crowds threw rotten food and rocks at them. Public punishment served two purposes: it punished the criminal and warned others not to break the law. Trials were often unfair. Poor people had no lawyers. Torture was used to force confessions. If a judge believed you were guilty, you were guilty — there was no appeal. The system was based on fear, not fairness.
questions: What was the pillory and how was it used?
What were two purposes of public punishment?
How were trials different in medieval times compared to today?

informationalTexts.title: Sanctuary: The Law of the Church
informationalTexts.text: In medieval Europe, churches offered sanctuary — a safe place where criminals could hide from arrest. If someone being chased by guards ran into a church and grabbed the altar, they were protected by church law. Even the king's soldiers could not drag them out. Sanctuary could last for weeks or even months while the church negotiated with authorities. This law existed because the church believed forgiveness was more important than revenge. However, sanctuary had limits. If you left the church grounds, you could be arrested immediately. Some criminals lived in churches for years, afraid to step outside. Notre-Dame's sanctuary plays a crucial role in Hugo's story.
questions: What does sanctuary mean in medieval church law?
How long could sanctuary last?
What happened if someone left the church grounds during sanctuary?

informationalTexts.title: The Roma People: Europe's Outcasts
informationalTexts.text: The Roma (sometimes called Gypsies, though many consider this term offensive) have lived in Europe for over 600 years. They originally came from India and traveled in caravans, working as musicians, dancers, metalworkers, and traders. Because they moved from place to place and had different customs, many Europeans feared and mistrusted them. Laws banned Roma from entering cities. They were accused of witchcraft and theft, often unfairly. In Hugo's novel, Esmeralda is Roma. Hugo shows how prejudice treats an entire group of people as criminals just because they are different. Understanding this history helps us see why the Roma characters in the story face such cruelty.
questions: Where did the Roma people originally come from?
What kinds of work did Roma people traditionally do?
Why were Roma people mistrusted in medieval Europe?

informationalTexts.title: Gargoyles: Monsters with a Purpose
informationalTexts.text: Gargoyles are strange, often frightening stone creatures that stick out from the sides of Gothic buildings. They look like dragons, demons, or twisted animals. But gargoyles weren't just decoration — they were actually waterspouts. Rain would flow through the gargoyle's mouth and shoot away from the building, protecting the walls from water damage. Medieval people also believed gargoyles scared away evil spirits. The word 'gargoyle' comes from an old French word meaning 'throat' or 'to gargle,' because of the gurgling sound water makes as it flows through them. In Hugo's story, gargoyles are Quasimodo's companions — silent, misunderstood, and serving a purpose most people don't appreciate.
questions: What practical purpose did gargoyles serve?
Where does the word 'gargoyle' come from?
What did medieval people believe about gargoyles and evil spirits?

informationalTexts.title: Deformity and Disability in the Middle Ages
informationalTexts.text: In medieval times, people with physical disabilities or deformities were often treated cruelly. Many believed that a twisted body meant a twisted soul, or that disability was punishment from God. People with deformities were sometimes abandoned as babies, locked away, or displayed as curiosities in traveling shows. Medical knowledge was limited — doctors didn't understand conditions like scoliosis (curved spine) or dwarfism. However, not everyone was cruel. Some monasteries and churches cared for people society rejected. A few disabled people became successful artists, scholars, or craftspeople. Hugo wrote Quasimodo to challenge the idea that physical appearance reflects inner worth. The novel asks: who is truly monstrous — the person who looks different, or the people who treat them cruelly?
questions: How were people with deformities often treated in medieval times?
What did many medieval people wrongly believe about disability?
What question does Hugo's novel ask about true monstrosity?

informationalTexts.title: The Court of Miracles: Paris's Hidden City
informationalTexts.text: The Court of Miracles was a real place in medieval Paris — a neighborhood where outcasts, criminals, and the poor lived together outside the law. It was called the 'Court of Miracles' because beggars who pretended to be blind, lame, or sick during the day would miraculously 'heal' when they returned home at night. The area was a maze of narrow alleys where city guards rarely dared to enter. It had its own rules, its own king, and its own sense of justice. Thieves and con artists mixed with genuinely poor families who had nowhere else to go. Hugo's novel shows the Court of Miracles as both dangerous and compassionate — a community of people rejected by the world above.
questions: Why was it called the 'Court of Miracles'?
Why didn't city guards often enter this area?
What kinds of people lived in the Court of Miracles?

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Dance and Music
informationalTexts.text: Music and dance were everywhere in medieval life. Traveling musicians called minstrels went from town to town playing lutes, drums, and flutes. Dances were performed at festivals, weddings, and markets. The tambourine was especially popular with Roma dancers. Dancing was a way for common people to celebrate and forget their hard lives for a while. However, the church often disapproved of dancing, seeing it as sinful and distracting people from prayer. Women who danced professionally, like Esmeralda in the story, were often viewed with suspicion. People thought dancers were wild and immoral. This prejudice made dancers vulnerable to accusations and arrest.
questions: What were traveling musicians called in medieval times?
Why did common people love music and dance?
Why were professional dancers often viewed with suspicion?

informationalTexts.title: The Power of the Church in Medieval Times
informationalTexts.text: In the Middle Ages, the Catholic Church was one of the most powerful institutions in Europe. It owned vast amounts of land, collected taxes called tithes, and had its own courts and laws. Church leaders like bishops and archdeacons were as powerful as nobles. A corrupt church official could destroy lives without fear of punishment. The church controlled education — almost all schools were run by priests. It also controlled what people read: books had to be copied by hand by monks, and the church decided which books were acceptable. People who questioned the church could be arrested for heresy (disagreeing with church teachings). This power is important in Hugo's story, where Frollo uses his church position to harm innocent people.
questions: What kinds of power did the medieval church have?
What was heresy?
Who controlled education and books in medieval times?

informationalTexts.title: Obsession: When Love Becomes Dangerous
informationalTexts.text: The word 'obsession' comes from a Latin word meaning 'to besiege' — like an army surrounding a castle. When someone is obsessed, their mind is under siege by a single thought or desire they cannot escape. Obsession is different from love. Love wants the other person to be happy. Obsession wants to possess and control. In Hugo's novel, Frollo is obsessed with Esmeralda. He doesn't love her — he wants to own her. When he can't have her, he wants to destroy her. Hugo shows how obsession can turn a person into a monster, regardless of how respectable they seem on the outside. Understanding obsession helps us see why Frollo is the story's true villain.
questions: What does the word 'obsession' originally mean in Latin?
How is obsession different from love?
Who is obsessed in Hugo's story, and with whom?

informationalTexts.title: Loyalty: Choosing Who You Serve
informationalTexts.text: Loyalty means staying faithful to a person, cause, or institution even when it's difficult. In medieval times, loyalty was everything. Knights swore loyalty to their lords. Servants were loyal to their masters. Breaking an oath of loyalty was one of the worst things a person could do. But Hugo's novel asks a harder question: what do you do when the person you're loyal to is wrong? Quasimodo has been loyal to Frollo his entire life. Frollo raised him, protected him, and gave him a home. But when Frollo commands Quasimodo to do something evil, Quasimodo faces an impossible choice. Sometimes true loyalty means disobeying orders when those orders are cruel.
questions: Why was loyalty so important in medieval times?
What difficult choice does Quasimodo face in the story?
Can disobeying an order sometimes be the loyal thing to do?

informationalTexts.title: Beauty and Ugliness: Who Decides?
informationalTexts.text: Every culture has ideas about what is beautiful and what is ugly. But these ideas are not universal — they change over time and place. In medieval Europe, pale skin was considered beautiful because it meant you didn't work in the fields. Plumpness was attractive because it meant you had enough to eat. Today's beauty standards are completely different! Hugo's novel challenges the idea that outer beauty reflects inner goodness. Quasimodo is physically deformed, but he is gentle, loyal, and brave. Frollo is handsome and respected, but his soul is twisted by obsession and cruelty. Esmeralda is beautiful, but her beauty makes her a target. The story forces readers to think about what true beauty really means.
questions: How have beauty standards changed over time?
What point is Hugo making about outer and inner beauty?
Give examples from the story of characters whose appearances don't match their souls.

informationalTexts.title: Mobs: When Crowds Turn Dangerous
informationalTexts.text: A mob is a large crowd of people who lose their individual judgment and act emotionally as a group. Mobs can form quickly and do terrible things that individuals would never do alone. In a mob, people feel anonymous and powerful. They stop thinking rationally. They feed off each other's emotions — anger, fear, or excitement — until the feeling becomes overwhelming. Mobs can be swayed by a single passionate speaker. Once a mob forms, it's extremely difficult to stop. Hugo shows this multiple times in his novel: the crowd that crowns Quasimodo and then mocks him, the mob that storms the cathedral, the people who cheer for executions. Understanding mob psychology helps explain how good people can participate in terrible acts.
questions: What is a mob?
Why do people in mobs do things they wouldn't do alone?
Give an example of mob behavior from Hugo's story.

informationalTexts.title: Redemption: Can People Change?
informationalTexts.text: Redemption means being saved from evil or error. It's about transformation — becoming a better person than you were before. Many stories explore whether people can truly change. Can someone who has done terrible things become good? In Christian theology (important in medieval times), redemption was central: anyone could be forgiven if they truly repented. But Hugo's novel asks whether redemption is possible for everyone. Quasimodo redeems himself through his protection of Esmeralda — he becomes more than the frightened, obedient creature Frollo raised. But can Frollo be redeemed? Or has he gone too far? The story suggests that redemption requires recognizing you were wrong — and some people are too proud or too far gone to do that.
questions: What does redemption mean?
How does Quasimodo redeem himself in the story?
Why might some people be unable to achieve redemption?

informationalTexts.title: Fate vs. Free Will
informationalTexts.text: Fate is the idea that everything is predetermined — that your life is already written and you can't change it. Free will is the belief that you make your own choices and control your own destiny. Medieval people often believed in fate. They thought God had a plan for everyone and trying to fight it was useless. Hugo explores this question throughout his novel. Is Quasimodo's life predetermined by his deformity and Frollo's control? Or does he have the power to choose his own path? When Quasimodo defies Frollo and chooses to protect Esmeralda, he asserts his free will. The novel suggests that even when life seems predetermined, moments of choice — moments of courage — can change everything.
questions: What is the difference between fate and free will?
What did medieval people tend to believe about fate?
When does Quasimodo assert his free will in the story?

informationalTexts.title: The Symbolism of the Cathedral
informationalTexts.text: In literature, a symbol is something that represents something else. Notre-Dame Cathedral is the central symbol in Hugo's novel. On one level, it's just a building — stone, wood, glass, and metal. But symbolically, it represents many things: permanence in a changing world, sanctuary and safety, beauty created by human hands, the soul of Paris itself. The cathedral shelters Quasimodo and later Esmeralda. It stands while people come and go, live and die. Hugo wrote the novel partly to save the real Notre-Dame from being destroyed. By making it the heart of his story, he reminded readers that some things — beauty, history, compassion — are worth preserving.
questions: What is a symbol in literature?
What does Notre-Dame Cathedral symbolize in the novel?
Why did Hugo make the cathedral so central to his story?

informationalTexts.title: Medieval Torture and Interrogation
informationalTexts.text: In medieval times, torture was a legal part of interrogation. Authorities believed that pain would force people to tell the truth. Common torture methods included the rack (stretching the body), the wheel (breaking bones), and the strappado (hanging by bound arms). Accused criminals were tortured until they confessed — even if they were innocent. Many people confessed to crimes they didn't commit just to make the pain stop. Medieval society believed torture was necessary for justice, but we now know it produces false confessions and is morally wrong. Hugo includes torture in his novel to show the cruelty of medieval justice and how power can be abused when one group has total control over another.
questions: Why did medieval authorities use torture?
What problem occurs when torture is used to get confessions?
What point is Hugo making by including torture in his story?

informationalTexts.title: The Seine River: Paris's Lifeline
informationalTexts.text: The Seine River flows through the center of Paris, dividing the city into the Left Bank and Right Bank. In medieval times, the river was essential for transportation, trade, and daily life. Boats carried goods up and down the river. Fishermen caught fish to sell in the markets. People drew water from the river for drinking, cooking, and washing (though this often made them sick). Notre-Dame sits on an island in the Seine, called Île de la Cité. This island was the original center of Paris. The river features prominently in Hugo's novel — it's where characters travel, hide, and sometimes meet their fate. Understanding the river's importance helps us see why the cathedral's location was so significant.
questions: What are the two banks of the Seine River called?
What was the river used for in medieval times?
On what island does Notre-Dame stand?

informationalTexts.title: Heroism: Courage in Impossible Situations
informationalTexts.text: A hero is someone who shows great courage, especially in the face of danger. But heroism isn't just about fighting dragons or winning battles. Sometimes heroism is protecting someone weaker than yourself. Sometimes it's standing up to authority when authority is wrong. Sometimes it's choosing compassion when everyone else chooses cruelty. Quasimodo is an unlikely hero. He's been taught to hide, to obey, to believe he's worthless. But when the moment comes, he acts with extraordinary courage. He defies Frollo, claims sanctuary for Esmeralda, and defends the cathedral against an army. His heroism is powerful because he had to overcome so much fear and self-doubt to find it. True heroes aren't fearless — they're afraid and act anyway.
questions: What makes someone a hero?
How is Quasimodo an unlikely hero?
What makes Quasimodo's heroism especially powerful?

informationalTexts.title: Tragedy: When Stories Don't End Happily
informationalTexts.text: A tragedy is a story where the hero suffers or dies, often because of forces beyond their control or their own flaws. Tragedies are different from stories with happy endings — they leave us sad, thoughtful, and asking hard questions. Why do tragedies exist? Because life doesn't always end happily. Sometimes good people suffer. Sometimes courage isn't enough. Tragedies teach us about the costs of pride, cruelty, and injustice. They make us think about what matters most. Hugo's novel is a tragedy — not everyone survives, and those who do are forever changed. But tragedies aren't pointless. They help us understand the world and make us want to create a better one.
questions: What is a tragedy in literature?
Why do authors write tragedies instead of happy endings?
What does Hugo's tragic ending make readers think about?

grammar.skill: Identify and write sentences with independent and dependent clauses
Use prepositional phrases to add detail (on the tower, under the cathedral, through the streets)
Use appositives to add information (Quasimodo, the bell ringer, lived alone)
Use who, which, that to connect ideas (The cathedral, which stood for 200 years, was beautiful)
Use -ing and -ed words to describe (The ringing bells, the frightened crowd)
Combine multiple clauses for sophisticated writing
Use semicolons to join related independent clauses
Use colons to introduce lists or explanations
Keep items in a series grammatically consistent
Identify and choose between active and passive constructions
Use subjunctive for wishes, suggestions, demands (If I were... I wish he were...)
Rearrange sentence order for dramatic effect (Never had he seen... Only then did she...)

language.skill: Cathedral structure, deformity, transform — build vocabulary from roots
Identify metaphors (The cathedral is the heart of Paris)
Understand word feelings (monster, creature, outcast have different emotional weights)
Words that change meaning by context (court: royal vs. courtyard)
Deformed, disfigured, misjudged — understand negative prefixes
Use like/as comparisons (as agile as a cat, like a gargoyle)
Identify author's attitude through word choice
Understand phrases that mean something different (turn a blind eye, heart of stone)
Understand when objects represent ideas (bells = voice, cathedral = permanence)
Sanctuary, benediction, dictate — holy and speaking words
Use surrounding text to figure out unfamiliar words

writing.prompt: Notre-Dame Cathedral took 200 years to build. Describe something in your life that took a long time to create or achieve. What made it worth the effort? (1-2 paragraphs)
Quasimodo is called a 'foundling' — a child abandoned and found by others. Write about a time you felt like you didn't belong somewhere. How did you handle those feelings? (1-2 paragraphs)
The Festival of Fools turns everything upside down for one day. If you could reverse one rule for a day, what would it be and what would happen? (1-2 paragraphs)
Esmeralda shows Quasimodo compassion when no one else does. Write about a time someone showed you unexpected kindness. How did it change things? (1-2 paragraphs)
Gargoyles are ugly but serve an important purpose. Write about something that doesn't look appealing but is actually very useful or valuable. (1-2 paragraphs)
The Court of Miracles is a community of outcasts who help each other. Why do people who have been rejected sometimes form the strongest communities? (1-2 paragraphs)
Frollo has power and uses it to hurt people. Write about why it's important to hold powerful people accountable for their actions. (1-2 paragraphs)
Quasimodo must choose between loyalty to Frollo and doing what's right. Have you ever had to choose between following orders and following your conscience? (1-2 paragraphs)
Mobs can make good people do bad things. Write about a time you saw (or experienced) peer pressure to do something wrong. What should people do in those situations? (1-2 paragraphs)
The novel asks whether we control our own fate. Write about a moment when you made a choice that changed something important. (1-2 paragraphs)
Medieval justice was cruel and unfair. Why is it important to have fair trials and treat accused people humanely, even if we think they're guilty? (1-2 paragraphs)
Quasimodo becomes a hero by protecting someone weaker than himself. Write about what heroism means to you. Does someone have to do something dramatic to be a hero? (1-2 paragraphs)

journal.prompt: Victor Hugo's book literally saved Notre-Dame Cathedral from destruction. Have you ever created something (art, writing, a project) that changed someone's mind or helped solve a problem?
Bell ringers lost their hearing from loud bells, but Quasimodo loved the bells anyway. Write about something you love even though it has difficult or painful parts.
Public punishments in medieval times were meant to shame people. Do you think shaming is an effective way to change behavior? Why or why not?
The Roma people were mistrusted because they were different. Write about a time when someone judged you unfairly based on how you looked or where you came from.
Medieval people wrongly believed that physical deformity meant a bad soul. What modern-day prejudices do you think future generations will look back on and find shocking?
Dancing and music helped people escape their hard lives. What activity helps you feel free or forget your worries for a while?
Obsession is different from love because it wants to control, not support. How can you tell if someone's feelings for you (friendship or more) are healthy or unhealthy?
Beauty standards change over time and culture. What do you think makes a person truly beautiful, beyond physical appearance?
Redemption means becoming better than you were. Write about a time you made a mistake and worked to become better. What did you learn?
Notre-Dame Cathedral symbolizes permanence — it stands while people come and go. What building, place, or object feels permanent and meaningful in your life?
The Seine River was essential to medieval Paris. What natural feature (river, mountain, lake, forest) is important to your community or region?
Tragedies don't have happy endings, but they teach important lessons. What's a book, movie, or real-life story with a sad ending that made you think differently about the world?

words.definition: A large, important church, often the main church of a city. Cathedrals are usually grand and ancient buildings.
words.sentence: Notre-Dame was more than a building — it was the heart of Paris, the place where kings were crowned and the city gathered in times of trouble.

words.definition: A carved stone creature, often ugly or monstrous, placed on the outside of a building to drain rainwater. Gargoyles also serve as decoration.
words.sentence: The gargoyles perched on every ledge and rooftop, staring down at the city with frozen expressions that seemed to be laughing at a joke no one else could hear.

words.definition: A baby or young child who has been abandoned by their parents and found by someone else.
words.sentence: The foundling bed was a place where parents who could not care for their babies left them, hoping the church would take them in.

words.definition: A part of the body that is an unusual shape or does not work normally. A physical difference that others may notice.
words.sentence: The priests recoiled from the baby's deformity — his hunched back, his enormous head, his twisted limbs.

words.definition: The state of being completely alone, especially for a long time. Isolation from other people.
words.sentence: Quasimodo lived in almost total solitude — his only companions were the bells, the gargoyles, and the pigeons.

words.definition: Able to move quickly and easily. Nimble and light on your feet despite physical challenges.
words.sentence: Despite his hunched body, Quasimodo was as agile as a cat — he could climb walls and leap between ledges that would terrify an ordinary person.

words.definition: An impressive or dramatic display that people watch. Also: something or someone being stared at, often in a humiliating way.
words.sentence: Quasimodo went from being celebrated as a spectacle to being mocked as one — the crowd's love turned to cruelty in an instant.

words.definition: Making rude, mocking comments or sounds at someone. Laughing at someone to hurt or embarrass them.
words.sentence: The cheering became jeering — the crowd threw rotten vegetables and mocked his hunched back and twisted limbs.

words.definition: A feeling of deep sympathy for someone who is suffering, combined with a desire to help them.
words.sentence: While the crowd mocked Quasimodo, Esmeralda showed compassion — she pushed through the mob, wiped the mud from his face, and treated him like a person.

words.definition: A sense of pride and self-respect. The quality of being worthy of honor and fair treatment.
words.sentence: Esmeralda restored Quasimodo's dignity by treating him as a human being when everyone else treated him as a joke.

words.definition: A person who has been rejected by society or a group. Someone who does not belong to the accepted community.
words.sentence: The Court of Miracles was home to everyone Paris had rejected — the outcasts who had nowhere else to go.

words.definition: To say that someone can be trusted. To take responsibility for another person.
words.sentence: Esmeralda vouched for the poet Gringoire, saving his life by taking responsibility for him even though she did not know him.

words.definition: The power to give orders and make decisions. The right to control others.
words.sentence: Frollo held absolute authority over the cathedral and everyone in it — he decided what was sin and what was virtue.

words.definition: Controlling someone through cleverness and deception rather than force. Making people do what you want without them realizing it.
words.sentence: Frollo's manipulation of Quasimodo was masterful — not with shouting, but with quiet, relentless control that made Quasimodo believe the world hated him.

words.definition: Doing what you are told without questioning. Following orders because you believe the person giving them has the right to command you.
words.sentence: Quasimodo obeyed Frollo's command to capture Esmeralda, even though something inside him whispered it was wrong.

words.definition: The inner sense of right and wrong. The small voice inside you that tells you when something is not right.
words.sentence: Something inside Quasimodo — a small, new feeling like a seed breaking through soil — whispered that capturing Esmeralda was wrong.

words.definition: A judge or official who has the power to make legal decisions and punish people.
words.sentence: Quasimodo was brought before a magistrate who saw a monster and sentenced him without listening.

words.definition: Judging someone before you know them, usually based on how they look or where they come from.
words.sentence: The judge's prejudice against Quasimodo's appearance made a fair trial impossible — he had decided guilt before hearing a word.

words.definition: The feeling of being made to look foolish or worthless in front of others. Public shame.
words.sentence: The pillory was designed to humiliate — the prisoner stood helpless while the crowd did its worst.

words.definition: Having no interest or concern. Not caring about someone's suffering.
words.sentence: Frollo stood in the crowd and turned away — indifferent to the suffering of the person he had raised.

words.definition: Compassion or forgiveness shown to someone you could punish or ignore. Choosing kindness when cruelty would be easier.
words.sentence: Esmeralda's cup of water was an act of mercy — she helped the person who had hurt her, not because he deserved it, but because he needed it.

words.definition: A deep feeling of thankfulness. Appreciation for kindness received, especially when unexpected.
words.sentence: The tear that rolled from Quasimodo's eye was not from pain but from gratitude — the overwhelming realization that kindness existed.

words.definition: A group of people who live together, share resources, and take care of each other.
words.sentence: The outcasts of Paris had built a real community in the Court of Miracles — with shared food, their own laws, and people who cared for each other.

words.definition: Innocent and trusting, sometimes to the point of not seeing danger. Lacking experience with the harsh parts of the world.
words.sentence: Esmeralda was not naive — she knew her freedom was fragile and that men like Frollo wanted it destroyed.

words.definition: Pretending to have beliefs or standards that you do not actually follow. Saying one thing and doing another.
words.sentence: Frollo's greatest hypocrisy was claiming to serve justice while building a tower of lies to destroy an innocent girl.

words.definition: Made up. Invented and presented as true even though it is false.
words.sentence: Frollo fabricated the witchcraft charges against Esmeralda — every piece of evidence was a lie.

words.definition: A claim that someone has done something wrong or illegal. A formal charge of wrongdoing.
words.sentence: The accusation of witchcraft was impossible to disprove — how do you prove you are NOT a witch?

words.definition: A person who watches something happen without getting involved. Someone who sees wrongdoing but does not act.
words.sentence: The crowd was full of bystanders — people who knew Esmeralda was innocent but were too afraid to speak.

words.definition: A safe place where someone is protected from harm. In medieval times, a person who reached a church could claim sanctuary and be legally untouchable.
words.sentence: Quasimodo carried Esmeralda into the cathedral and shouted the word that would protect her: SANCTUARY!

words.definition: Openly refused to obey someone in authority. Stood against a powerful person or rule.
words.sentence: The hunchback had defied Frollo — for the first time in his life, he made a choice his master did not make for him.

words.definition: A safe place to go when you are in danger. Shelter from threats.
words.sentence: The cathedral became a true refuge — not just for Esmeralda's body but for Quasimodo's spirit.

words.definition: Something that fills you with wonder and amazement. To be astonished by something extraordinary.
words.sentence: Esmeralda marveled at the view from the tower — all of Paris laid out below her like a painting.

words.definition: Something you believe to be true without proof. A judgment you make before having all the facts.
words.sentence: The crowd assumed Quasimodo was a monster because of his appearance — they never looked deeper.

words.definition: Very unusual, remarkable, or exceptional. Far beyond what is ordinary or expected.
words.sentence: Esmeralda told Quasimodo that the most extraordinary people in the world are often the most ordinary-looking — and the reverse is also true.

words.definition: A final demand or statement of terms — do this, or face consequences. A threat with a deadline.
words.sentence: Frollo delivered an ultimatum: Esmeralda must leave the cathedral, or he would find a way to take her by force.

words.definition: The process of being set free from control or oppression. Gaining independence.
words.sentence: Quasimodo's refusal to hand over Esmeralda was his emancipation — the moment he stopped being Frollo's puppet and became his own person.

words.definition: An attack where forces surround a place and try to force the people inside to surrender.
words.sentence: Frollo sent fifty soldiers to lay siege to the cathedral and take Esmeralda by force.

words.definition: Breaking a rule, law, or sacred right. Acting against something that should be protected.
words.sentence: Attacking the cathedral was a violation of sanctuary — a right older than any law Frollo could claim.

words.definition: Inspiring fear or respect through being impressively large, powerful, or capable.
words.sentence: Quasimodo was formidable in defense — one man held off fifty soldiers for three hours using stones, boiling water, and sheer determination.

words.definition: The quality of not giving up. Stubborn determination to keep going no matter what.
words.sentence: Quasimodo's tenacity amazed the people of Paris — he was bleeding, exhausted, and outnumbered, but he refused to stop.

words.definition: Betrayal of trust. Acting against someone who trusted you, especially through deception.
words.sentence: Frollo's treachery was complete — he used Esmeralda's exhaustion against her, pretending to offer help while leading her into a trap.

words.definition: You learned this word in Chapter 13 (Musketeers). Frollo uses deception here — pretending to help Esmeralda while actually leading her into a trap.
words.sentence: Frollo's deception was chilling — he made his voice almost human, almost kind, as he led Esmeralda to the waiting soldiers.

words.definition: A direct, face-to-face conflict between opposing people. The moment when tensions finally come to a head.
words.sentence: The rooftop confrontation between Quasimodo and Frollo was sixteen years in the making — the foundling finally facing the man who had raised and controlled him.

words.definition: A time when someone must face the consequences of their actions. A moment of judgment and truth.
words.sentence: Frollo's reckoning came on the rooftop — the place where he had kept Quasimodo imprisoned for sixteen years became the place where his power finally ended.

words.definition: Being proven right or innocent after being accused or doubted. The clearing of someone's name.
words.sentence: Esmeralda's vindication came when Frollo's own papers proved her innocence — every charge had been fabricated.

words.definition: You learned this in Chapter 8. Now the city of Paris is experiencing a collective conscience — the uncomfortable realization that they allowed injustice to happen.
words.sentence: The people were horrified by what Frollo's papers revealed. Their conscience — too quiet when it mattered — now screamed.

words.definition: A complete change in appearance, character, or situation. Becoming something fundamentally different.
words.sentence: The bells rang with a new sound — not the dutiful ringing of a servant, but the free expression of a heart that had been transformed by friendship.

words.definition: The feeling of being accepted and valued as part of a group. Knowing that you have a place in the world.
words.sentence: For the first time, Quasimodo felt a sense of belonging — not because the world had changed, but because he had found a friend who saw him truly.

questions.question: What two celebrations are happening in Paris at the start of the story?
options: Christmas and New Year
The Feast of Epiphany and the Festival of Fools
The King's birthday and a wedding
Easter and a harvest festival

questions.question: The chapter ends by telling us that someone watches the festival from high in the cathedral tower but 'could not go down.' What mood does this create? What do you already feel about this character before you even know his name?
Why does Frollo take in baby Quasimodo?
options: Because he genuinely loves the baby
Because it suits his idea of duty, and a dependent orphan will owe him everything
Because the king orders him to
Because the other priests insist

questions.question: Frollo tells Quasimodo that the outside world is cruel and dangerous. Is Frollo trying to protect Quasimodo, control him, or both? Use details from the chapter to support your answer.
Why does Quasimodo love the gargoyles?
options: They are valuable and made of gold
They look like him and never flinch from his face
Frollo told him to take care of them
They can talk to him

questions.question: Quasimodo watches the people of Paris from the tower and wishes he could join them. What does this tell you about what he wants most in life? Is it something that can be given to him, or must he find it himself?
What happens when the crowd realizes Quasimodo's face is not a costume?
options: They feel sorry for him and help him down
They cheer even louder
The celebration turns cruel — they mock him, throw things, and call him a monster
They ignore him and move on

questions.question: The crowd celebrates Quasimodo and then immediately turns on him. Why do you think this happens? What does this scene teach about how groups of people behave differently than individuals?
What does Esmeralda do when she finds Quasimodo being mocked by the crowd?
options: She joins in the mocking
She ignores him and walks away
She pushes through the crowd, wipes the mud from his face, and treats him with kindness
She calls for the soldiers

questions.question: Esmeralda is the only person who helps Quasimodo. Everyone else either mocks him or does nothing. Why do you think she acts when no one else does? What makes her different?
How does Pierre Gringoire end up in the Court of Miracles?
options: He is invited by Esmeralda
He wanders in by accident while lost after his failed play
He is captured by soldiers
Frollo sends him there as a spy

questions.question: The Court of Miracles is described as a community where outcasts take care of each other. How does this challenge the way the rest of Paris sees these people? What does the Court reveal about who the 'real' monsters are?
Why does Frollo see Esmeralda as a threat?
options: She is physically dangerous
She has an army
Her kindness and the people's love for her challenge Frollo's authority and control
She has magical powers

questions.question: Frollo tells Quasimodo: 'I am the only one who has ever cared for you.' Is this true? Is what Frollo provides actually care, or is it control disguised as care? How can you tell the difference?
Why does Quasimodo agree to capture Esmeralda even though he senses it is wrong?
options: He wants to hurt her
He has been trained his entire life to obey Frollo without question
He does not care about her
He is bribed with money

questions.question: Quasimodo feels something 'like a seed breaking through soil' telling him this is wrong, but he obeys Frollo anyway. Why is it so hard to disobey someone you have always obeyed? What would it take for Quasimodo to finally choose his own conscience over Frollo's commands?
Why can't Quasimodo defend himself at the trial?
options: He refuses to speak
He is nearly deaf from years of bell-ringing and cannot hear the questions, and his speech is rough and halting
The judge does not allow him to speak
He admits guilt immediately

questions.question: Quasimodo cannot name Frollo as the person who gave the order. Why not? Is this loyalty, fear, or trained obedience? What does it cost Quasimodo to protect Frollo?
What does Quasimodo see when he looks for Frollo in the crowd?
options: Frollo rushing to help him
Frollo arguing with the judge
Frollo turning his back and walking away
Frollo is not there at all

questions.question: The chapter says something 'broke inside Quasimodo' when Frollo turned away. What broke? Why is this moment more painful than the stones and the jeering?
Why does Esmeralda bring water to the person who tried to capture her?
options: She is forced to by the soldiers
She wants something from him
She believes cruelty should be answered with kindness, not more cruelty
She does not recognize him

questions.question: This chapter is the turning point of the entire story. A single cup of water changes Quasimodo forever. How can such a small act have such enormous power? What does the water represent beyond just a drink?
What does Clopin warn Esmeralda about?
options: That her dancing is not good enough
That being kind is more dangerous than being careful
That Quasimodo will betray her
That the festival is cancelled

questions.question: Clopin says: 'You are kind. That is more dangerous.' Why would kindness be dangerous in a city ruled by someone like Frollo? What does kindness threaten that cruelty does not?
Why does Frollo decide to destroy Esmeralda?
options: She committed a real crime
He is afraid of losing control — her kindness challenges his authority and has awakened Quasimodo's independence
She attacked him
The king orders him to

questions.question: Frollo tells himself his anger is 'righteous' — that he is protecting Paris from a threat. Do you think he believes his own lies? Can a person convince themselves they are doing good when they are actually doing harm?
How does the crowd react when Esmeralda asks them to speak up for her?
options: They defend her loudly
They are silent — everyone is too afraid of Frollo to speak the truth
They attack the soldiers
They help her escape

questions.question: Every person in the crowd knows Esmeralda is innocent, but no one speaks up. The chapter says: 'Power works not by convincing people wrong is right, but by making them too afraid to speak the truth.' Do you agree? Can you think of other examples where fear silences people?
What motivates Quasimodo to rescue Esmeralda?
options: Frollo tells him to
He wants a reward
The memory of her kindness at the pillory — one cup of water changed everything
Captain Phoebus asks him to

questions.question: Quasimodo makes his first independent decision in this chapter — to rescue Esmeralda. He goes against Frollo, against the soldiers, against everything he has been taught. What does this moment reveal about who Quasimodo really is inside?
What does Quasimodo show Esmeralda in the cathedral?
options: Nothing — he avoids her
Hidden passages, the bells, the gargoyles, and the view of Paris from the top of the tower
Only his room
Frollo's study

questions.question: Quasimodo says: 'I never had anyone to share it with.' Why is sharing something important — even something you see every day? How does having a friend change Quasimodo's experience of the cathedral?
Why does Quasimodo keep looking away from Esmeralda?
options: He is angry at her
He is ashamed of his face and afraid she must be disgusted by it
He cannot see well
He is bored

questions.question: Esmeralda says: 'The outside of a person tells you nothing about the inside.' This is the central message of the entire book. Do you agree? Use examples from the story — and from real life — to explain your thinking.
How does Quasimodo respond when Frollo demands that Esmeralda be turned over?
options: He agrees and hands her over
He says 'no' — for the first time in his life defying Frollo to his face
He runs away
He asks Esmeralda to decide

questions.question: Quasimodo says: 'No one will take you from this cathedral. I promise.' This is the first promise he has ever made to anyone other than Frollo. Why is this promise so significant? What has changed inside Quasimodo?
What does Quasimodo shout down at the soldiers?
options: He surrenders immediately
He calls for Frollo to help him
He bellows that Esmeralda has sanctuary and the law forbids them to enter
He asks the crowd for help

questions.question: One person against fifty soldiers seems impossible. What makes Quasimodo willing to fight those odds? What does he have that the soldiers don't?
How do the people of Paris react as they watch Quasimodo defend the cathedral?
options: They help the soldiers break in
They are astonished — they thought he was a monster, but now they see courage and love
They ignore the fight completely
They cheer for Frollo

questions.question: The people of Paris have always seen Quasimodo as a monster. Now they see him fighting alone to protect someone. How does this moment change their understanding of who he is? What lesson does it teach about judging people?
How does Frollo trick Esmeralda into leaving the cathedral?
options: He uses force
He pretends to offer help and safe passage, then has soldiers waiting outside the door
He bribes her
He convinces Quasimodo to make her leave

questions.question: Esmeralda follows Frollo even though she does not trust him. She is tired of hiding and tired of being a prisoner. Does this make her foolish, or is it understandable? When is it hardest to keep your guard up?
What does Quasimodo say to Frollo on the rooftop?
options: He begs Frollo to forgive him
He says: 'You are the monster' — finally seeing Frollo clearly for the first time
He says nothing
He agrees to obey Frollo again

questions.question: Quasimodo tells Frollo: 'You cannot hurt me. You have hurt me every day for sixteen years, and I survived.' What does this moment mean for Quasimodo? How is he different from the person who obeyed Frollo without question at the beginning of the story?
What proves Esmeralda's innocence?
options: A witness comes forward
Frollo's own private papers reveal every fabricated charge, bribery, and abuse of power
The king pardons her
Quasimodo confesses

questions.question: Esmeralda watches the crowd cheer for her freedom and feels 'not joy exactly, but something more complicated.' She notes these are the same people who said nothing when she was arrested. Why is her response more complicated than simple happiness? What has she learned about people?
Why do the bells of Notre-Dame sound different at the end of the story?
options: They have been repaired and retuned
A different person is ringing them
Quasimodo is ringing them with joy for the first time — his inner transformation changes the music
The weather has changed the sound

questions.question: The story ends with Paris 'learning, one day at a time, to be kind.' This is not a fairy-tale ending where everything is perfect. Why did the author choose this realistic ending instead of a happily-ever-after? What message does it send about how the world actually changes?
